findings.summary: requests is a hard base dependency but is only used by the optional LLM client
evidence: pyproject.toml line 42: 'requests>=2.31.0  # Required by infrastructure/llm/core/client.py (base dep, not llm optional group)'
The comment itself acknowledges this is a known placement compromise — requests is pulled into every install even without LLM use
findings.suggestion: Move requests into the llm dependency group and guard the import in client.py with a try/except ImportError, consistent with how ollama, plotly, and reportlab are handled

findings.summary: Six separate test files exist for pdf_renderer, indicating coverage-chasing rather than domain-driven test decomposition
evidence: 6 test files for a single production module, with names like '_coverage', '_missing_coverage', '_additional', '_fixes' — suffix names that signal incremental patch files rather than designed test suites
Pattern repeats in validation: test_check_links.py, test_check_links_comprehensive.py, test_check_links_edge_cases.py, test_check_links_full.py — 4 files for one module
Same pattern in rendering CLI: test_rendering_cli.py and test_rendering_cli_full.py
findings.suggestion: Consolidate patch test files into a single well-organized test module per production module. Use pytest marks (slow, integration) to segregate test types rather than separate files with suffixes like _full, _coverage, _additional

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py (623 LOC) and projects/code_project/scripts/optimization_analysis.py (1649 LOC) are untested with zero importers
evidence: Blind packet existing_findings: 'scripts/01_run_tests.py: test_coverage: Untested critical module (623 LOC, 0 importers) — high blast radius'
Blind packet existing_findings: 'projects/code_project/scripts/optimization_analysis.py: test_coverage: Untested critical module (1649 LOC, 0 importers) — high blast radius'
01_run_tests.py is the primary test orchestration entry point — not tested despite being 623 LOC
findings.suggestion: Extract testable logic from 01_run_tests.py into infrastructure modules (e.g., suite_runner.py already exists for this purpose) and write tests against the extracted functions. For optimization_analysis.py, apply the thin-orchestrator pattern strictly — all computation should move to projects/code_project/src/ where 90% coverage is enforced

findings.summary: Five near-identical failure-parsing functions with mechanical suffixes (_section, _verbose, _short, _timeout, _fallback) indicate AI-generated strategy proliferation
evidence: Lines 35-130: _parse_failures_section, _parse_failures_verbose, _parse_failures_short, _parse_failures_timeout, _parse_failures_fallback — five functions with identical signatures (combined_output: str) -> list[dict]
Each is 10-20 lines with a single regex loop; the distinctions are format-specific but the code structure is copy-paste with minor variation

All five are collected into _FAILURE_STRATEGIES list and iterated in order — the strategy dispatch is not wrong, but generating 5 separate named functions rather than a parameterized approach is characteristic AI fragmentation
findings.suggestion: Parameterize the strategy dispatch: each strategy is a regex pattern + extractor, representable as a data table (pattern, field_extractor) rather than five named function bodies. This would halve the LOC and make adding new formats a data change, not a code change

findings.summary: Trivial __init__ docstrings that restate the class name add no value
evidence: error_aggregator.py line 51: '"""Initialize error aggregator."""' on a 2-line __init__ that sets two empty lists
output_organizer.py line 91: '"""Initialize the OutputOrganizer."""' — same pattern
Neither docstring conveys any information beyond the class name already visible in the class definition
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings from trivial constructors that do nothing but initialize instance variables; the class-level docstring already describes the object's purpose

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ (14 files, 8902 LOC) bundles test-runner infrastructure, coverage parsing, pipeline reporting, executive summaries, and dashboard generation — four distinct concerns in one package
evidence: suite_runner.py streams pytest subprocess output — this is test execution infrastructure, not reporting
coverage_parser.py calls subprocess to run pytest --help to detect flags — test orchestration, not reporting
dashboard_generator.py is 3462 LOC generating matplotlib/plotly charts — visualization, not reporting
executive_reporter.py (1415 LOC) generates cross-project metrics aggregation — analysis, not reporting in the narrow sense
The directory's avg_fan_in is 3.4 (moderate) but internal file count and LOC are the heaviest in the codebase aside from validation/
findings.suggestion: Split infrastructure/reporting/ into at minimum: infrastructure/reporting/ (pipeline_reporter, coverage_reporter, output_reporter, error_aggregator, html_templates), infrastructure/reporting/runners/ or infrastructure/testing/ (suite_runner, coverage_parser, suite_summary_generator), and either keep dashboard_generator co-located or move it to infrastructure/visualization/
